did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an Attention Enhanced Convolution Net (AECNet), a deep learning model, for skin cancer classification. It also employed an Auxiliary Classifier Generative Adversarial Network (ACGAN) to generate synthetic data for training and Grad-CAM as an Explainable AI (XAI) technique to interpret the model's predictions on dermatological images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, introducing a novel model (AECNet) and methodology (ACGAN-based synthetic data generation) for skin cancer classification. It includes a methodology section, experimental results with specific performance metrics (e.g., 95.63% test accuracy), and a conclusion summarizing new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: AECNet: Advancing Skin Cancer Classification using ACGAN-Based Synthetic Data with Grad-CAM Explainability
journal: 2025 2nd International Conference on Next-Generation Computing, IoT and Machine Learning (NCIM)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer classification, particularly melanoma, by introducing the Attention Enhanced Convolution Net (AECNet), a hybrid attention-based CNN model. To mitigate data scarcity and class imbalance, the authors employed an Auxiliary Classifier Generative Adversarial Network (ACGAN) to generate synthetic dermoscopic images for training. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which was split into training and testing sets, achieving a test accuracy of 95.63%. The inclusion of synthetic data improved the model's generalization and classification performance compared to versions without it. Additionally, Grad-CAM was utilized as an Explainable AI technique to visualize the regions of interest, enhancing clinical interpretability and trust in the model's predictions.

lesion_types_studied.details: Class 1 in the binary classification task; described as the most fatal type of skin cancer. The dataset included 8057 Class 1 images after ACGAN augmentation.
Class 0 in the binary classification task; grouped as non-melanoma. The dataset included 7609 Class 0 images after ACGAN augmentation.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into Melanoma (Class 1) and Benign (Class 0)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and benign skin lesions using the AECNet model on the ISIC 2018 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant/melanoma) with a reported test accuracy of 95.63%, precision, recall, and F1-score. The task is explicitly defined as 'skin cancer classification' and 'differentiating benign from malignant'.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Challenge 2018 dataset, used for skin cancer diagnosis involving segmentation, clinical attribute identification, and disease classification in dermoscopic pictures.

use.training: Used as the primary source for training the AECNet model. The original training set (2550 benign, 3028 melanoma) was augmented with synthetic images generated by ACGAN to create a final training set of 7609 Class 0 and 8057 Class 1 images.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation. The test set consisted of 860 benign and 1010 melanoma images from the original split, which were later augmented to 2536 Class 0 and 2686 Class 1 images for evaluation purposes.
use.other: The dataset was split with a 0.25 testing split ratio. It served as the basis for generating synthetic data via ACGAN to address class imbalance and data scarcity.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the agreement between the model's predictions and the ground truth, assessing robustness and reliability.
To measure the quality of binary classifications, assessing robustness and reliability.
To evaluate model performance across various criteria, specifically measuring similarity between predicted and actual sets.
To perform error analysis, comparing true positives, false negatives, true negatives, and false positives between models with and without synthetic images.
To evaluate the performance of the model at various threshold settings and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for both benign and melanoma classifications.
To compare the performance metrics (accuracy, loss, etc.) of the AECNet model with and without synthetic data augmentation, and against other state-of-the-art methods.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models with and without synthetic data were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, loss, Cohen’s Kappa, etc.) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018 Dataset.notes: The inclusion of synthetic images generated by ACGAN significantly improved performance metrics compared to the model without synthetic data. The model with synthetic data achieved an AUC of 0.99 for both classes, indicating almost perfect differentiation capability. Grad-CAM visualizations showed that the model with synthetic data emphasized larger and more intense areas for melanoma class prediction, while stressing less intense and more localized areas for the benign class.

architecture_details.model_family: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with hybrid attention mechanism
architecture_details.channel_attention: Global Average Pooling and Global Max Pooling followed by a Shared Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with sigmoid activation
architecture_details.spatial_attention: Average and Max Pooling along channel dimension, concatenated, followed by convolution with sigmoid activation
architecture_details.pooling_layers: MaxPooling and Global Pooling (Average and Max)

training_details.early_stopping: Based on validation loss with patience of 10 epochs
training_details.loss_function: Softmax classification (implied by output layer), Adversarial Loss (BCELoss) and Auxiliary Loss (CrossEntropyLoss) for ACGAN generator/discriminator

classification_or_output_head.description: Classifies input as benign or malignant

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SNNs (Spiking Neural Networks) with spiking VGG-13
preprocessing_techniques: Label mapping to divide images into benign (Class 0) and melanoma (Class 1) groups
Resizing images to 28x28x3 dimensions using bilinear interpolation
Normalization of pixel values scaling from [0, 1]
Conversion of images to NumPy arrays
Mixing and shuffling synthetic and real photos every epoch

data_augmentation_techniques.details: An Auxiliary Classifier Generative Adversarial Network (ACGAN) was used to generate synthetic dermoscopic images to address data scarcity and class imbalance. The generator concatenated random noise with class labels to create class-specific images. Key parameters included: Adam optimizer, batch size of 16, 60 iterations, learning rate of 0.0001, adversarial loss (BCELoss), auxiliary loss (CrossEntropyLoss), momentum terms b1=0.5 and b2=0.999, and input image size of 224x224x3. The generator used Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU activations, outputting 224x224x3 RGB images. The discriminator used convolutional layers, binary cross-entropy loss, and predicted class labels simultaneously.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Mixing and Shuffling of Synthetic and Real Images
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Synthetic images generated by ACGAN were mixed with real images from the training set. These mixed datasets were shuffled every epoch during training to ensure a good balance across classes and improve model generalization.

conclusions: The proposed Attention Enhanced Convolution Net (AECNet) model achieved a test accuracy of 95.63% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, outperforming models without synthetic data augmentation (87.01% accuracy) and other state-of-the-art methods such as DGAN (92.3%) and CNN with Image Super-Resolution (87.5%).

The integration of Auxiliary Classifier Generative Adversarial Network (ACGAN) generated synthetic data significantly improved model generalization, robustness, and classification performance, as evidenced by improvements in Cohen’s Kappa (0.9126 vs 0.7372), Matthews Correlation Coefficient (0.9127 vs 0.7384), and Jaccard Score (0.9163 vs 0.7696).

The AECNet model with synthetic data achieved an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.99 for both benign and melanoma classifications, indicating almost perfect differentiation capability compared to the 0.95 AUC achieved without synthetic data.

Grad-CAM explainability analysis demonstrated that the model with synthetic data effectively focuses on relevant regions of interest, emphasizing larger and more intense areas for melanoma predictions and less intense, localized areas for benign predictions, thereby enhancing clinical interpretability and trust.

Error analysis revealed that the model with synthetic data significantly improved benign case detection (2403 true positives vs. 712 without synthetic data) and melanoma detection (2591 true negatives vs. 915 without synthetic data), reducing misclassification of benign cases as melanoma.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the need for clinical validation and cooperation with healthcare practitioners for practical acceptance, and identifies future work priorities including expanding datasets, investigating advanced GAN architectures, extending to multi-class classification, and developing real-time, deployable systems with multi-modal data integration.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Melanoma vs. Benign) rather than multi-class skin cancer diagnosis, restricting its immediate clinical applicability to broader dermatological contexts.
The model was evaluated exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset; the lack of external validation on independent datasets raises concerns about generalizability and potential dataset bias.

Synthetic images generated by ACGAN were mixed with real training data and shuffled every epoch; while intended to improve generalization, this approach requires careful scrutiny to ensure no data leakage occurred between the synthetic generation process and the test set.

The paper acknowledges that clinical validation and cooperation with healthcare practitioners are necessary for practical acceptance, indicating that the current results are pre-clinical and not yet validated in a real-world clinical setting.

The input image size for the AECNet model was reduced to 28x28x3, which may result in the loss of fine-grained dermoscopic details crucial for accurate skin cancer diagnosis compared to higher-resolution inputs.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the significance of performance improvements; comparisons were based on descriptive metrics alone.

The study is a conference paper presented at NCIM 2025, which may have less rigorous peer review standards compared to top-tier journals, warranting caution in interpreting the robustness of the findings.
